paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1184 English Language — AI-authored Simplified Practice 33: The Rooftop Garden (§B style, simplified band)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary 2 girl who helps a lonely elderly neighbour, Mr Chandran, tend a small rooftop garden on their HDB block, and who keeps the garden alive after he is moved into a nursing home. Answer the questions that follow. Section B [15 marks] — AI-authored simplified practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1184/02 (formerly 1128) Section B format and calibrated to the SEAB assessment objectives (literal / inferential comprehension, vocabulary in context, language for effect), pitched at students stretching from middle secondary toward the full O-Level standard. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. Items mix mark values [1]/[2]: 7 short-answer questions (Q1-Q7) plus a 4-blank emotion-flowchart MCQ (Q8). Answer in your own words as far as possible.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
Mr Chandran had lived three floors above us for as long as I could remember. His wife had passed away years before, and his children worked overseas, so he spent most of his days alone. On the roof of our HDB block, in a corner few people visited, he kept a small garden of chilli plants, curry leaves and pandan, growing in old paint tins and cracked clay pots.

Paragraph 2
I first went up there because my mother asked me to return a dish his helper had left at our door. I found him kneeling among the pots, his hands dark with soil. When he asked if I wanted to help water the plants, I almost said no — I was in Secondary 2 and felt I had better things to do on a Saturday. But something in his tired smile made me stay.

Paragraph 3
After that, I went up every weekend. Mr Chandran taught me how to pinch off the dead leaves, how to tell when the chillies were ready, and how much water the pandan liked. He told me stories about his kampung days while we worked. Slowly the rooftop became my favourite place, and the lonely old man became my friend.

Paragraph 4
Then, one morning in March, an ambulance came. Mr Chandran had fallen in his flat, and after that his children decided he could no longer live on his own. Within a month he was moved to a nursing home in the east, far across the island. The rooftop garden was suddenly quiet, and the pots sat waiting for hands that would not come.

Paragraph 5
For a while I stopped going up. What was the point? The plants were his, not mine, and looking at them only reminded me of how empty the roof now felt. My mother said no one would blame me if I let the garden go. Twice I climbed the stairs from the void deck meaning to clear the pots away for good.

Paragraph 6
But each time I stood at the door to the roof, I could not do it. I remembered his soil-dark hands and his kampung stories. I thought of how proud he had been of every green shoot. Letting the garden die felt like letting a part of him die too. So I picked up the watering can instead.

Paragraph 7
All through the long months I kept the plants alive. I pulled the weeds, chased away the mynahs, and learned from my mistakes whenever a plant drooped. Then, one Saturday in the June holidays, I gathered our first real harvest — a handful of red chillies and a thick bunch of pandan leaves, still wet with the morning dew.

Paragraph 8
That afternoon my mother took me on the long bus ride to the nursing home. I brought a photograph of the harvest, laid out neatly on the rooftop tiles. When Mr Chandran saw it, his eyes grew bright and he held the picture in both hands. 'You kept it going,' he whispered. In that moment I knew that some things are worth holding on to, even when it would be easier to let them go.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1184 §B style practice, simplified-band difficulty; not from a past exam. HDB: Housing and Development Board public-housing flats; void deck: the open ground floor of an HDB block; kampung: a traditional village of old Singapore.)

questions.stem: Q1. From Paragraph 1, name two of the plants that Mr Chandran grew in his rooftop garden. [1]
questions.answer: any two of: chilli, curry leaves, pandan

questions.stem: Q2. What does the word 'tired' in 'his tired smile' (Paragraph 2) suggest about Mr Chandran? [1]
questions.answer: he was weary and lonely, worn down by living on his own

questions.stem: Q3. Using your own words, explain why the narrator almost said no when Mr Chandran first asked her to help. (Paragraph 2) [2]
questions.answer: ① she was a Secondary 2 student who felt she had better / more interesting things to do; ② she did not want to spend her Saturday helping an old man she hardly knew

questions.stem: Q4. What does the word 'whispered' (Paragraph 8) suggest about how Mr Chandran spoke when he saw the photograph? [1]
questions.answer: he spoke very softly, too full of emotion / almost moved to tears

questions.stem: Q5. Using your own words, explain why the narrator stopped going up to the roof for a while. (Paragraph 5) [2]
questions.answer: ① she felt the plants were his and not hers, so there seemed no point; ② seeing them only reminded her how empty and lonely the roof now was without him

questions.stem: Q6. What is the effect of the phrase 'the pots sat waiting for hands that would not come' (Paragraph 4)? [2]
questions.answer: it gives the pots human feeling, as if they are abandoned and longing — making the garden's emptiness and the loss of Mr Chandran feel sad and real

questions.stem: Q7. Using your own words, explain why the narrator could not clear the pots away. (Paragraph 6) [2]
questions.answer: ① the garden reminded her of Mr Chandran — his soil-dark hands and his kampung stories; ② letting it die felt like letting a part of him die too

sections.instruction: Q8. The narrator's main feeling shifts as her time with the rooftop garden unfolds. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: reluctant / joyful / heartbroken / hopeful / jealous / furious / bored / impatient. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'The Rooftop Garden' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift as the story unfolds — from reluctant in Paragraph 2, when she almost refuses to help because, as a Secondary 2 student, she feels she has better things to do on a Saturday, to joyful in Paragraph 3, when she goes up every weekend, listens to his kampung stories, and the rooftop becomes her favourite place while the lonely old man becomes her friend, to heartbroken in Paragraphs 4 and 5, when Mr Chandran is moved to a nursing home far across the island, the garden falls quiet, and she is tempted to clear the pots away for good, ending hopeful in Paragraphs 6 to 8, when she decides to keep the garden alive, gathers the first harvest, and brings Mr Chandran a photograph of it. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q8(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 2, when Mr Chandran first asks her to help water the plants and she almost says no because, as a Secondary 2 student, she feels she has better things to do on a Saturday? [1]

Q8(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 3, when she goes up every weekend, learns to tend the plants, listens to his kampung stories, and the rooftop becomes her favourite place while the lonely old man becomes her friend? [1]

Q8(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 4 and 5, when Mr Chandran falls, is moved to a nursing home far across the island, the rooftop garden falls quiet, and she twice climbs the stairs meaning to clear the pots away for good? [1]

Q8(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 6 to 8, when she decides to keep the garden alive, gathers the first harvest of chillies and pandan, and brings Mr Chandran a photograph that makes his eyes grow bright? [1]
